g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive lesson on the OSI Model, the foundational framework for understanding network communication.
g.text: Open Systems Interconnection - 7 Layers of Network Communication

g.data-narration: The OSI Model is a conceptual framework developed by ISO in 1984 that standardizes network communication into seven abstract layers.
g.text: A conceptual framework that standardizes network communication functions into 7 layers.
Developed by ISO in 1984, it enables interoperability between different computer systems.

g.data-narration: The seven layers are: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, and Physical. Use mnemonics to remember them easily.

text.tspan: "All People Seem To Need Data Processing"
"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"

g.data-narration: Layer 1 Physical deals with raw binary data transmission through cables, connectors, hubs, and network interface cards.
g.text: Transmits raw binary data (0s and 1s) over cables and wireless media.

g.data-narration: Layer 2 Data Link provides node-to-node transfer with error detection, framing, and MAC addressing for local network communication.
g.text: Node-to-node data transfer with error detection using CRC.
Devices: Switch, Bridge | Protocols: Ethernet (802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11)

g.data-narration: Layer 3 Network handles logical addressing and routing of packets across different networks using IP addresses.
g.text: Enables logical addressing and routing packets across networks.
Devices: Router | Protocols: IP, ICMP, IGMP, OSPF, BGP

g.data-narration: Layer 4 Transport ensures complete data transfer with segmentation, flow control, and port addressing using TCP or UDP protocols.
g.text: End-to-end communication with segmentation and flow control.
TCP: Reliable, connection-oriented | UDP: Fast, connectionless
Port numbers: HTTP(80), HTTPS(443), FTP(21), SSH(22), DNS(53)

g.data-narration: Layer 5 Session manages establishment, maintenance, and termination of sessions with checkpointing for reliable data transfer.
g.text: Manages sessions between applications - establishment, maintenance, termination.
Synchronization with checkpoints enables recovery if connection fails.
Protocols: NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP | Dialog control: simplex, half-duplex, full-duplex

g.data-narration: Layer 6 Presentation acts as a translator handling data formatting, encryption, decryption, and compression between systems.
g.text: Data translation and format conversion - ensures data from one system is readable by another.
Functions: Encryption/Decryption (SSL/TLS), Compression (JPEG, MPEG), Character encoding (ASCII)
Standards: JPEG, GIF, PNG, ASCII, EBCDIC, SSL/TLS, MPEG

g.data-narration: Layer 7 Application provides network services to user applications through protocols like HTTP, FTP, SMTP, and DNS.
g.text: Interface between user applications and the network. Provides network services.
Protocols: HTTP/HTTPS (web), FTP (file transfer), SMTP (email send), DNS (name resolution)
Note: This layer is NOT the application itself, but the protocols applications use!

g.data-narration: Network devices operate at specific OSI layers: hubs at Layer 1, switches at Layer 2, routers at Layer 3, and gateways at all layers.
Each OSI layer uses specific Protocol Data Units: Bits at Physical, Frames at Data Link, Packets at Network, Segments at Transport.
TCP provides reliable, connection-oriented communication with error recovery. UDP offers fast, connectionless communication without guarantees.
g.text: Reliable, Connection-oriented | Error recovery | Flow control | 3-way handshake
Fast, Connectionless | No error recovery | No flow control | Best for VoIP, DNS, Video
Use TCP for: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SSH, Email (SMTP/POP3/IMAP)
Use UDP for: DNS, DHCP, VoIP, Video streaming, Online gaming

g.data-narration: Data encapsulation adds headers at each layer as data flows down: Application adds data, Transport adds ports, Network adds IP, Data Link adds MAC.
In summary, the OSI Model with its 7 layers provides a standardized framework for understanding and troubleshooting network communication.
g.text: 1. OSI Model has 7 layers: Physical → Data Link → Network → Transport → Session → Presentation → Application
2. Each layer adds its own header (encapsulation) and communicates only with adjacent layers.
3. MAC addresses (Layer 2) are physical, IP addresses (Layer 3) are logical, Ports (Layer 4) identify applications.
4. TCP is reliable (connection-oriented), UDP is fast (connectionless). Use based on application needs.

g.data-narration: For troubleshooting, use the OSI model systematically: check cables at Layer 1, MAC at Layer 2, IP at Layer 3, ports at Layer 4, and DNS at Layer 7.
